title: Conjecture 5 (Fraughnaugh and Locke)
statement_text: If $G$ is a triangle-free subcubic $n$-vertex graph containing none of $F_{11}$, $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F^{(1)}_{19}$, $F^{(2)}_{19}$, $F_{22}$ as a subgraph, then $\alpha(G) \geq \frac{3}{8} n$.
context_text: Fraughnaugh and Locke formulated this conjecture after observing that the six graphs $F_{11}, F^{(1)}_{14}, F^{(2)}_{14}, F^{(1)}_{19}, F^{(2)}_{19}, F_{22}$ are the only known 2-connected triangle-free subcubic graphs failing $\alpha(G) \geq \frac{3}{8}n$. The conjecture implies both Conjecture 4 of Bajnok and Brinkmann and the earlier planar result proved by Heckman and Thomas, since none of the six forbidden graphs are planar.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture in full as the main result of the paper (Theorem 7).
paper_title: Large independent sets in triangle-free cubic graphs: beyond planarity

title: Conjecture 4 (Bajnok and Brinkmann)
statement_text: There are exactly six 2-connected triangle-free subcubic $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with $\alpha(G) < \frac{3}{8} n$, namely $F_{11}$, $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F^{(1)}_{19}$, $F^{(2)}_{19}$, $F_{22}$.
context_text: Bajnok and Brinkmann investigated the 2-connectivity conjecture of Fraughnaugh and Locke via computer search. Finding no counterexample, they identified exactly six exceptional graphs and conjectured these are the only ones.
paper_contribution: Confirms the conjecture as Corollary 8, a direct consequence of the paper's main theorem (Theorem 7).
paper_title: Large independent sets in triangle-free cubic graphs: beyond planarity

title: Conjecture 3 (Fraughnaugh and Locke)
statement_text: Let $G$ be a 2-connected triangle-free subcubic $n$-vertex graph. Then, $\alpha(G) \geq \frac{3}{8} n - \frac{1}{4}$.
context_text: Fraughnaugh and Locke conjectured this quantitative improvement over their Theorem 2 (which gives $\alpha(G) \geq \frac{11}{30}n - \frac{2}{15}$ for connected graphs) under the additional hypothesis of 2-connectivity. Bajnok and Brinkmann independently investigated it via computer search and found no counterexample.
paper_contribution: Confirms the conjecture as part of Corollary 8, a consequence of the paper's main theorem.
paper_title: Large independent sets in triangle-free cubic graphs: beyond planarity

title: Locke's Conjecture (1986)
statement_text: There are only finitely many 3-connected triangle-free cubic $n$-vertex graphs $G$ with $\alpha(G) < \frac{3}{8} n$.
context_text: Locke conjectured in 1986 that the bound $\alpha(G) \geq \frac{3}{8}n$ fails for at most finitely many 3-connected triangle-free cubic graphs. This conjecture motivated the subsequent stronger conjectures of Fraughnaugh and Locke and of Bajnok and Brinkmann under 2-connectivity.
paper_contribution: Corollary 8 implies the conjecture: since none of the six exceptional graphs are 3-connected, in fact no 3-connected triangle-free subcubic graph has $\alpha(G) < \frac{3}{8}n$, a stronger conclusion.
notes: The paper does not explicitly claim to prove Locke's 1986 conjecture; the implication follows from Corollary 8 and the observation that the six exceptional graphs are not 3-connected.
paper_title: Large independent sets in triangle-free cubic graphs: beyond planarity
